claims.text: In the space of just three weeks, the UN Human Rights Office documented 15 drone strikes on El Obeid, which killed at least 45 civilians. El Obeid has more than half a million residents, together with at least 100,000 internally displaced persons, and the number of newly displaced people across Kordofan has risen by nearly two-thirds in only three months.

G7 Foreign Ministers' joint statement on El Obeid, Sudan (14 July 2026): condemns ongoing RSF drone strikes on El Obeid and obstruction of humanitarian access, references the precedent of atrocities in El Fasher, and calls for an immediate ceasefire and compliance with the Jeddah Declaration to protect civilians.

NRC report (posted 7 July 2026) on El Obeid: families are dying from drone strikes and from hunger as the siege continues, with an aid convoy struck the preceding week; essential services and food access are collapsing further as the blockade persists.

UN News (17 July 2026): the UN human rights chief warned that civilians in El Obeid "have been under siege for 18 months" and face continuous drone strikes, with more than 100,000 displaced people sheltering in camps in the city. WFP is providing food assistance to over 100,000 people in those camps, including nutrition support for 17,000 children, but is distributing only a reduced ration that recipients are sharing with other families. Almost 20 million people in Sudan are now classified as acutely food insecure, and more than 14 million people have been uprooted nationwide since the conflict began.

WFP Geneva Palais briefing note (published 17 July 2026): El Obeid city (population approximately 500,000) is hosting displaced people, with more than 120,000 people in the 'Unified IDP Camp' alone. WFP is providing food/cash assistance to just over 100,000 people in camps inside the city, but severe funding shortfalls mean recipients are only receiving 50 percent of the ration they should. Fuel scarcity has crippled partner logistics, forcing WFP to deploy its own fleet vehicles to keep distributions running.

UNFPA Flash Update #2 on the situation in El Obeid, North Kordofan, Sudan (published 20 July 2026, covering 1-19 July): 21 drone-related incidents were recorded in and around El Obeid between 1-19 July 2026, causing civilian casualties (no aggregate death toll given), damage to critical infrastructure, and further disruption to already-limited essential services, with severe constraints on sexual and reproductive health and gender-based-violence response services due to insecurity and resource shortages.

description: Rapid Support Forces (RSF) paramilitary forces have besieged El Obeid, the capital of North Kordofan state, subjecting the city of more than half a million residents to relentless drone strikes and blockade conditions. As of 20 July 2026, the siege continues with no ceasefire and no ground assault yet confirmed by authoritative sources, but drone strikes have intensified (21 incidents recorded 1-19 July per a UNFPA Flash Update on ReliefWeb, up from 15 strikes over three weeks in the prior UN Human Rights Office count) and the humanitarian situation has worsened: WFP reports more than 120,000 people are now sheltering in the city's Unified IDP Camp alone, with food rations cut to 50 percent of full entitlement due to funding shortfalls. No new aggregate civilian death toll has been issued since the UN's 3 July report of at least 45 killed. G7 foreign ministers condemned the siege on 14 July 2026, and UN officials continue to warn of the risk of large-scale atrocities similar to those already documented in El Fasher, Darfur.

impact.summary: No updated aggregate death toll has been published since the 3 July UN Human Rights Office figure (15 drone strikes over 3 weeks, at least 45 civilians killed); drone strikes have continued and even increased in frequency (21 drone-related incidents logged 1-19 July per a UNFPA Flash Update via ReliefWeb), but no authoritative source has issued a new cumulative casualty count as of 22 July 2026. On displacement, WFP (17 July briefing) confirms more than 120,000 people are now in the Unified IDP Camp alone in El Obeid, up from the previously reported '100,000+ displaced in city camps.' Rations for WFP-assisted families have been cut to 50 percent of the full entitlement due to funding shortfalls, a downgrade from the earlier 'reduced rations' description. No ceasefire, siege-lifting, or ground assault has been confirmed by an authoritative source.

event.scale: City under siege; UN human-rights atrocity-risk warning

name: Siege of El Obeid — North Kordofan, Sudan
